verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (HSP60) is canonically a mitochondrial matrix chaperonin, but has well-documented non-baseline surface pools. On tumor cells and stressed cells, HSP60 translocates to the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane — this cancer/stress ecto-form is extensively documented in surface proteomics, flow cytometry, and IHC on intact tumor cells. HSP60 surface display has also been reported during apoptosis and immunogenic cell death, where it serves as a danger signal. Additionally, activated immune cells (particularly monocytes/macrophages) display HSP60 on the cell surface. Anti-HSP60 antibody and CAR-T programs have been designed to target the tumor-cell surface pool. Thus, while the dominant baseline localization is mitochondrial matrix, a documented cancer/stress/cell-death-induced ecto-pool firmly supports contextual surface accessibility.
